coredata.dc:title: An excitation wavelength-dependent color-tunable La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor for multicolor anticounterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Kui
Si, Changdai
Lv, Lingling
Li, Huixue
Zhang, Jianbin
coredata.dc:description: Stimulation-responsive luminescent materials have attractd intense attention due to their excellent optical properties in the anticounterfeiting application. At present, most the anticounterfeiting materials are based on single luminescence and fixed excitation patterns, resulting in a poor anticounterfeiting effect. Therefore, developing multimodal anticounterfeiting materials that change their luminous color under different wavelength stimuli has become a significant challenge. In this work, we reported a multimodal luminescent La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor, which could emit white, blue-green, baby blue and orange-yellow light at the excitation wavelength of 250–430 nm. Moreover, it also showed green upconversion (UC) luminescence when irradiated with near-infrared (NIR, 980 nm and 808 nm) lasers. Notably, the material exhibited good humidity resistance and stability. Based on these integrated features, we designed an anticounterfeiting device. The results suggested that the anticounterfeiting device could quickly realize multimodal anticounterfeiting using common light source {NIR (808 nm or 980 nm) laser and ultraviolet (UV) lamp}. These combined characteristics will be difficulty to forge and show high security in anticounterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multimodal
Multicolor
Down conversion
Upconversion
Anticounterfeiting

The UCPL properties of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of LGO: xBi3+, y Er3+ (x = 0, 0.03; y = 0, 0.014) and the standard PDF card of LGO. (b) XRD refinement of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor. (c) XPS spectra of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor. (d) The crystal structure of LGO and three different coordination sites of La. (e) The fine XPS spectra of Bi 4 f5/2, 4 f7/2 and Er 4d5/2.
The LGO: Bi3+, Er3+phosphor: (a-b) SEM images; (c) particle sizes distribution; (d) HRTEM image; (e) Elemental mapping distribution;(f) EDS spectrum.
DR spectra of the LGO host and LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor; Inset: experimental bandgap.
the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor (a) PLE spectra and(b) PL spectra; (c) PL spectra of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+sample relying on the excitation wavelengths.
The CIE color coordinates of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ sample at different wavelengths irradiation.
(a-b) UC spectra of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor at different laser (980 nm and 808 nm) power. (c-d) Data linear fitting yield the photon conversion number.
the UC energy level diagram of Er3+ at the irradiation of 980 nm and 808 nm laser.

(a) The multicolor variation of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor at the different excitation wavelength irradiation. (b) The color of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor under indoor light, 254 nm and 365 nm. (c) The UC photographs at the different pumping power (980 nm and 808 nm).
Anticounterfeiting application of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+phosphor as security ink.

head.ce:title: An excitation wavelength-dependent color-tunable La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor for multicolor anticounterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianshui Normal University, Tianshui 741001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianshui Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianshui Normal University, Tianshui 741001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimulation-responsive luminescent materials have attractd intense attention due to their excellent optical properties in the anticounterfeiting application. At present, most the anticounterfeiting materials are based on single luminescence and fixed excitation patterns, resulting in a poor anticounterfeiting effect. Therefore, developing multimodal anticounterfeiting materials that change their luminous color under different wavelength stimuli has become a significant challenge. In this work, we reported a multimodal luminescent La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor, which could emit white, blue-green, baby blue and orange-yellow light at the excitation wavelength of 250–430 nm. Moreover, it also showed green upconversion (UC) luminescence when irradiated with near-infrared (NIR, 980 nm and 808 nm) lasers. Notably, the material exhibited good humidity resistance and stability. Based on these integrated features, we designed an anticounterfeiting device. The results suggested that the anticounterfeiting device could quickly realize multimodal anticounterfeiting using common light source {NIR (808 nm or 980 nm) laser and ultraviolet (UV) lamp}. These combined characteristics will be difficulty to forge and show high security in anticounterfeiting applications.

A multimodal luminescent La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+phosphor
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Stimulation-responsive luminescent materials, whose colors or optical properties can be changed when irradiated with visible light, UV and NIR light, etc. [1–4], have attracted widespread attention due to their potential application in optical information storage [5,6], sensors [7,8] and anticounterfeiting [9,10], etc. Up to now, a large number of stimulation-responsive luminescence materials, such as metal-organic framework, inorganic luminescence materials, organic dyes and perovskites, etc. have been reported [11–18]. These luminescence materials can respond to external stimuli through color changes, and their emission wavelength can be tuned by changing chemical structures or appropriately dopants [19–21]. However, these stimulation-responsive luminescent materials usually show unicolor photoluminescent and fixed excitation patterns, and there are a few reports on excitation wavelength-dependent multicolor emission. Meanwhile, this unimodal emission and fixed excitation pattern have become increasingly acquainted with counterfeiters and easily imitated. Therefore, developing novel stimulation-responsive multimodal luminescent materials has become a challenging topic.

With in-depth research in the field of anticounterfeiting, researchers have been bending themselves to the development of multiple stimulus-response materials. For example, Shen [22] et al. reported a silicon nanoparticle that could produce multicolor emission at the different excitation wavelengths irradiation. Shi and his collaborators developed a dual-mode anticounterfeiting material with excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) performances while producing afterglow [23]. Wang [24] et al. developed a trimodal perovskite material that could simultaneously have the properties of afterglow, UC and downshifting (DS). etc. Compared with traditional static luminescent materials [25–27], these multiple stimulus-responsive materials have additional safety characteristics due to their color-tunable, difficulty in copying and large encoding capacity. However, these reported luminescent materials still existed some deficiencies. For example, the common UV sources (254 nm and 365 nm) can’t excite; the color change is not apparent, or smaller excitation band, etc. These shortcomings seriously limit their application in practical anticounterfeiting. Thus, developing excitation wavelength-dependent multimodal luminescent materials for security anticounterfeiting remains difficulties.

Here, we have successfully developed a novel excitation wavelength-dependent multimodal luminescent La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor, with a relatively wide excitation band (200 nm) and could be detected under common UV lamp. Our strategy considered that the naked 6 s electrons of Bi3+ ions ([Xe] 4 f145d106 s2) were sensitive to the surrounding coordination environment. Therefore, Bi3+ ions were incorporated into the La4GeO8 host for realizing multicolor emissions under UV light, while Er3+ was the selected activator for generating luminescence when irradiated with NIR laser. Results indicated that our design strategy was perfect. The La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor could emit white, blue-green, baby blue and orange-yellow at the different UV wavelength excitation. Moreover, the phosphor could also realize green UC luminescence under the NIR laser (980 nm or 808 nm) irradiation. In proof-of-concept experiments, the material showed good humidity resistance and stability. Furthermore, the La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+phosphor was applied in anticounterfeiting. The results showed that the device could quickly realize multimodal anticounterfeiting. These combined characteristics will be difficult to forge and provide high security in anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor
ce:section.ce:para: The La4−x-yGeO8:xBi3+, yEr3+ (x = 0.03, y = 0. 14) phosphor was synthesized by a simple solid-state combustion method. Using La2O3(A.R.), GeO2(A.R.), Bi2O3(99.99%) and Er2O3(99.99%) as starting materials, all raw materials were weighed according to the molar ratio of chemical composition La3.83Bi0.03Er0.14GeO8. These raw materials were ground evenly in agate mortar with ethanol. Afterward, the as-prepared mixture was placed in alumina crucibles and heated at 1300 ℃ for 4 h in a muffle furnace. After the muffle furnace naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained phosphor ground into fine powder for testing and characterizing.

The anticounterfeiting devices (QR codes) were fabricated by the screen-printing method. 0.8 g La4GeO8: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphors, 1.3 g Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and 0.13 g curing agent were evenly blended in a petri dish with a diameter of 60 mm. and then the mixture was coated on the QR code molds. Finally, the QR code mold was placed in an oven at 60 ℃ and dried for 2 h. The anti-counterfeiting pictures were obtained using MI 6X mobile phone under a UV lamp and NIR laser irradiation (980 nm and 808 nm).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on a Rigaku D/Max-2400 X-ray diffractometer. The Rietveld XRD refinement was performed using the GSAS software. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by FLS‐920 T fluorescence spectrophotometer. The up conversion (UC) spectra were recorded with the FLS‐920 T fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with external 980 nm and 808 nm laser. The diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were recorded with a PE lambda 950 UV−Vis spectrophotometer and BaSiO4 as standard. The mapping and energy dispersive spectra were recorded by a FEI TecnaiF30 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The valence states were identified by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI-5702 instrument).

The phase purity and composition of all the phosphors were investigated in detail. Fig. 1a depicted the XRD patterns of La4GeO8 (abbreviation as LGO) host and all different doped phosphors as well as the standard La4GeO8 (PDF No. 40–1185) card. It was found that all the diffraction peaks of undoped and doped samples could be well assigned to the standard card, which indicated that Bi3+ ions and Er3+ ions had been incorporated into the LGO host. To verify the influence of doped Bi3+and Er3+ to phase purity, the Rietveld refinement XRD patterns of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+samples were performed by the GSAS software. As shown in Fig. 1b, the observed peaks were well fitted through the refinement and converged to Rwp= 11.69%, Rp = 8.91%, which ensured that dopants could not cause phase changes, and the obtained LGO: Bi3+, Er3+phosphor was a purity phase. The detailed refinement parameters of undoped and doped LGO phosphor were depicted in Table S1. The unit cells crystal structure of the LGO host is orthorhombic system with space group P1. As shown in Fig. 1d, there were three types of La. The La1 sites coordinated with O to form a distortional octahedron.

[LaO6], while the La2 and La3 sites formed a decahedron [LaO7]. According to the Hume-Rothery rules [28], the La3+ (r = 1.032 Å, CN=6 and r = 1.1 Å, CN=7) should be replaced by Bi3+(r = 1.03 Å, CN=6) and Er3+(r = 0.89 Å, CN=6 and r = 0.945 Å, CN=7).

Because the chemical valence of Bi and Er considerably affected luminescence performances, identifying the valency of Eu in the LGO matrix is essential. As displayed in Fig. 1c, The XPS spectra exhibited characteristic peaks corresponding to La 3d, Ge 2p, O 1 s, Bi 4 f and Er 4d. Fig. 1e displayed the fine XPS spectra of Bi 4 f and Er 4d, and it was found that the binding energies of Bi 4 f (159 eV, 164.6 eV) and Er 4d (182 eV) were indexed with the signals of Bi3+ 4 f 7/2, 4 f 5/2 and Er3+ 4d5/2, thereby demonstrated that Bi and Er coexist in LGO host in the trivalent state [29,30].

The morphology and particle sizes of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor were shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. 2c. The phosphor comprises out-of-shape particles with sizes ranging from 0.14 to 3.0 µm. The HRTEM image was displayed in Fig. 2d, the interplanar spacing was measured to 1.326 Å, corresponding to the (3 10 2) interplanar distance of the LGO host. Based on the elemental mapping distribution analysis in Fig. 2e, Bi and Er were evenly distributed in the LGO host. The EDS spectra were given in Fig. 2f, which confirmed that the signals of La, Ga, O, Bi and Er were highly consistent with the chemical composition of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+.

The bandgap (Eg) of LGO matrix and LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor could be calculated by the following formula: [31].
(1) (αhν)2=A (hν- Eg)

hν is the incident photon energy, α is the absorption coefficient，and A is a constant, where α can be obtained via the Kubelka–Munk function: [32,33] ·
(2)
α =
( 1 −
R
2
)
2 R

R is the observed reflectance in the DR spectra. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3, the pure LGO host has an indirect bandgap (Eg≈ 3.89 eV), when dopants were incorporated into the LGO matrix, the bandgap dramatically decreases and the Eg was 2.63 eV, indicating that dopants considerably change the original properties of LGO and make electrons easier excited from the valence band maximum and the conduction band. Meanwhile, the necessary conditions are provided for obtaining a color-tunable emission depending on the excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The DSPL properties of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor was further investigated by PLE and PL spectra in detail. As presented in Fig. 4a, regardless of monitoring 470 nm, 605 nm or 619 nm, the PLE spectra showed two prominent broad peaks at 325 nm and 393 nm in the range of 250–500 nm, of which the strong absorbed peak at 393 nm was attributed to the 1S0–3P1 transition of Bi3+, while the other located at 325 nm were ascribed to the 1S0–1P1 transition. According to the previous reports, the energy levels of Bi3+ in the LGO matrix could also be calculated based on the following equation: [34,35].
(3) EC=3·236+2·290(EA-2·972)0.865

EA and EC represent the energy of 1S0–3P1 and 1S0–1P1 transitions of Bi3+, respectively. EA= 3.16 eV (393 nm). Hence, the calculated EC was about 3.78 eV (329 nm), which indicated that the band at 325 nm was attributed to the 1S0–1P1 transition of Bi3+. Under the different wavelengths (310 nm, 370 nm and 390 nm), the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor showed tunable multicolor emission, and the PL exhibited three distinct broad bands at 400–560 nm, 540 ~710 nm and 560 ~ 680 nm, respectively (Fig. 4b). Results indicated that these broad emission peaks were caused by the spin-allowed 3P1 –1S0 transition of Bi3+ in different La sites. Because the sensibility of Bi3+ naked 6 s electrons to the crystal field and the different excitation efficiency of Bi3+ at different wavelengths irradiation, so we could obtain color-tunable emission depending on the excitation wavelength. As.

exhibited in Fig. 4c, it was clearly observed that the intensity and position of emission bands in the spectrum revealed remarkable changes under the varied excitation wavelength irradiation. As a result, the phosphor presented white, blue-green, baby blue and orange-yellow light (Fig. 8a), which indicated that the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor showed an obvious multicolor feature at different ultraviolet source irradiation (250–430 nm). The DSPL energy level diagram is shown in Fig. S4. Under different excitation wavelengths, electrons in the ground state (1S0) were excited to the 3P1, 3P2, and 1P1 energy level of Bi3+ ions, the excited electrons in the 3P1 energy level got back to the ground state through the radiative way. Simultaneously, Part of electrons populating in 1P1 and 3P2 level transferred to 3P1 energy level by nonradiative transition, which will emit direct photoluminescence.
In order to make the optical performance of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor more visualized, we calculated the CIE (1931) of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphors under the different excitation wavelength.

As shown in Fig. 5, the CIE coordinate changed with excitation wavelength, and the corresponding colors gradually turned from white, blue-green, baby blue to orange-yellow, which further indicated that the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+phosphor had the advantages of color-tunable luminescence. Table S2 listed the CIE chromaticity coordinate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The UCPL properties of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the UCPL performance of the as-prepared LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphors, the UCPL intensities-dependent laser (980 nm and 808 nm) power was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6a, As the laser (980 nm) pumping power was increased, the UCPL intensity increased progressively more potent, and its color changed gradually from hidden to bright (Fig. 8c). To further investigate the number of photon conversion in the UC process, the UC emission intensities of Er3+ ions at 535 nm and 550 nm versus pumping power (980 nm) were studied according to the following equation: [36,37].
Iup∝P n

Iup is emission intensity, P is pumping power, and n is photon conversion. As shown in Fig. 6c, the fitting yielded straight lines with a slope of 1.81 (535 nm) and 1.88 for (550 nm), respectively. Suggesting that the UC was a two-photon conversion process involved 2H11/2→4I15/2 and5S3/2→4I15/2 transition of Er3+ ions [38]. Similarly, the UC spectra of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ samples along with the laser power (808 nm) were displayed in Fig. 6b, the slope by linear fitting was 1.92(535 nm) and 1.86 (545 nm), respectively, which suggested that the UC also obeyed a two-photon conversion process.

Furthermore, the corresponding UCPL energy level diagram. is shown in Fig. 7. The electrons were excited from the 4I15/2 level to the excited state 4I11/2 through ground-state absorption (GSA)under 980 nm NIR excitation. Later, the excited state electrons are again transferred to the 4I13/2, 4I11/2, 2F9/2 and 2F7/2 energy levels of Er3+ by the excited state absorption (ESA). Due to the instability of electrons populating the metastable energy level of Er3+, some electrons jump to excited metastable 2H11/2 and 4S3/2 levels through the non-radiative transition and cross-relaxation (CR) process. Finally, these electrons in the metastable levels of Er3+ relax to the ground state in the form of green emission through 2H11/2 → 4I15/2 and 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 transitions. In addition, the luminescence mechanism of 808 nm excited upconversion is similar to that of the 980 nm, except that, the electrons were excited from the ground state towards 4I9/2. After that the ions relax to the 4I11/2.

To evaluate the feasibility of LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ materials in harsh terms, a series of tests about stability and water resistance were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5 and S6. Firstly, stability was explored by placing this material in the dark on different days. The results indicated that the luminescent intensity remained relatively stable. Second, the water-resistance of phosphor was.

studied by placing it in tap water for some time and the results showed that the intensities decline of PL/UCL is not significant, which demonstrated that the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ materials possessed excellent performance against harsh terms Fig. 8.

Inspired by the color-tunable multimode performance, the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+ phosphor was fabricated QR code anticounterfeiting device. As shown in Fig. 9, the QR code was invisible under natural conditions. However, the code showed.

different color when used a portable UV lamp (254 nm or 365 nm) or NIR laser (980 nm or 808 nm) to irradiate, which demonstrated that the LGO: Bi3+, Er3+materials possessed excellent anti-counterfeiting ability.

In summary, we have successfully prepared the Bi3+ /Er3+ co-doping La4GeO8 phosphor for multicolor anticounterfeiting. Under the irradiation of different wavelengths ultraviolet sources, the material could emit white, blue-green, baby blue and orange- yellow light, which was ascribed to the sensibility of Bi3+ naked 6 s electrons to the crystal field and the different excitation efficiency of Bi3+ and Er3+ at different wavelength irradiation Meanwhile, it also exhibited green UC luminescence at the irradiation NIR laser (980 nm or 808 nm). The material showed good stability and water resistance in practical application after being composited with other materials. Based on these integrated features, a multifunctional anticounterfeiting device was fabricated. Results suggested that the anticounterfeiting device could quickly realize multimodal anticounterfeiting using a familiar UV lamp (254 nm or 365 nm) or NIR laser (980 nm or 808 nm). The characters will be tough to fake and shows high-level security in anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 22163008), Tianshui Normal University Innovation Ability Enhancement Project (XT2019-15)，Tianshui NSF (2019-FZJHK-9258).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.164836.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
